slides.title: Too Many Functions 🤯
slides.content:
## Code Organization

If you put every single function you write inside `main.go`, your file will quickly become thousands of lines long and impossible to read.

To solve this, we group related functions into separate folders and files called **Packages**.

slides.title: The 'fmt' Package 📦
slides.content:
## We've Been Doing It!

Remember `fmt.Println()`? 

*   `fmt` is a package provided by Go.
*   `Println` is a function inside that package.

By saying `import "fmt"`, we load all of `fmt`'s functions into our program so we can use them!

quizData.question: What is the primary purpose of Go Packages?

options.text: To organize related code/functions and allow for easy reuse
To restrict access to the internet

quizData.explanation: Packages act as folders to neatly arrange your project's logic.

slides.title: Exporting Functions 🚪
slides.content:
## The Capitalization Rule

If you write a function in a separate package, how do you allow `main.go` to see it and use it?

**In Go, any function name that starts with a Capital Letter is PUBLIC (Exported).**
Any function that starts with a lowercase letter is PRIVATE to that package and hidden from the rest of the world.

*   `func Calculate()` -> Anyone can use this!
*   `func calculate()` -> Only other code in this specific package can see this.

quizData.question: If you want other packages to be able to use your custom function, you MUST start the function's name with an Uppercase letter.
quizData.explanation: Capitalization determines visibility in Go. Uppercase equals public/exported.
